ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for roselle rice salad. FOODON_00002809 (rice dish) used as closest parent. Roselle (Hibiscus sabdariffa) salad component is not individually coded in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Rice-based dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for roselle rice salad in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for roselle rice salad or sufficiently close Thai rice salad dish.

main_ingredients: Thai dressing (fish sauce, lime juice, chilli, garlic)
allergens: gluten (possible from soy sauce or seasoning)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of roselle rice salad with fried chicken is approximately 350 g, comprising a base of cooked rice mixed with roselle leaves or calyces, topped with a portion of fried chicken and dressed with a Thai-style lime-fish sauce dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Cooked white rice (SR Legacy #20045), Fried chicken (SR Legacy #05062), Roselle raw (SR Legacy #09307), and Thai salad dressing components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for roselle rice salad exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice (~45% by weight), fried chicken (~30% by weight), roselle raw (~10% by weight), and Thai dressing components (fish sauce, lime juice, chilli, ~15% by weight). Proportions are based on typical Thai restaurant salad construction. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed rice-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for roselle rice salad. GI of 56 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice (GI ~64) adjusted downward to ~56 to account for the presence of roselle (Hibiscus sabdariffa) polyphenols and dietary fibre, fried chicken protein and fat (which slow gastric emptying), and acidic Thai dressing (lime juice, vinegar-like components lower postprandial glucose response). This is consistent with published evidence that mixed rice dishes with protein, fat, and acidic dressings have GI values in the medium range (50–60). GL per serving = (56 × 61.25 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 34.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium (~47% of the 2300 mg daily limit), primarily from fish sauce and seasoning in the Thai dressing; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index from white rice base with partial mitigation from protein, fat, and roselle polyphenols
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 56 and GL of 34 per serving, this dish presents a moderate glycaemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion (150–200 g), substitute brown rice if available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from fried chicken and fish sauce dressing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fried chicken component contributes saturated fat (~1.3 g per 100 g) and the fish sauce dressing contributes significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should opt for grilled chicken substitution and reduced-sodium dressing to align with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roselle Rice Salad with Fried Chicken (Thai: สลัดข้าวกระเจี๊ยบแดงพร้อมไก่ทอด) is a contemporary Thai salad dish featuring cooked white rice as a base, combined with roselle (Hibiscus sabdariffa) leaves or calyces, and topped with crispy fried chicken. The dish is dressed with a classic Thai-style vinaigrette of fish sauce, fresh lime juice, chilli, and garlic, which imparts a bright, tangy, and mildly spicy flavour profile. The roselle component contributes a distinctive tart, cranberry-like flavour and a vivid red-pink colour, along with dietary fibre, vitamin C, and anthocyanin polyphenols that have been associated with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The fried chicken provides substantial protein and a contrasting crispy texture. As served at The Upper House – Funan in Singapore, this dish represents a modern Thai-fusion interpretation priced at SGD 18. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy mixed meal (~148 kcal per 100 g) with meaningful protein (~8.2 g per 100 g) and a medium glycaemic index (~56), making it a relatively balanced option compared to plain fried rice. However, the sodium content from fish sauce (~310 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and halal-compatible when prepared with standard Thai ingredients.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for roselle rice salad exists in any primary approved regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database data for white rice with documented adjustments. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice, fried chicken, raw roselle, and Thai dressing components. No single approved-source entry for this dish exists. 2. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for protein, fat, fibre, and acidic dressing components. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimate. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting trace amounts from the fried chicken component; this is not confirmed zero. 5. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on typical Thai restaurant main-course portions; actual restaurant portion may vary. 6. The input record listed food_group as 'Vegetables'; this has been corrected to 'Mixed' as the dish contains substantial rice and fried chicken components. 7. Halal status is assumed based on standard Thai restaurant preparation; pork-derived fish sauce variants would affect this flag. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite and are -1 only for vitamin_d_mcg which is not reported in the source database.
